title: Tapping for Loneliness Inside Marriage
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the specific isolation of feeling alone when you are not technically alone
excerpt: An EFT tapping practice for the loneliness that exists inside a relationship: the feeling of being with someone and still alone, of being side by side without being together. Works through the disconnect and the guilt that comes with naming it.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when you are in a relationship and feeling isolated within it: when the parallel life has gone on long enough that the connection feels like a story you used to be in.

content.text: A five-minute EFT tapping practice for loneliness inside a long-term relationship. The script works through the specific quality of this isolation, the guilt of naming it, and the gap between the life being lived and the one that was expected.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though I feel lonely inside my marriage and I don't quite know how we got here, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though naming the loneliness feels like a betrayal, I am open to being where I am with it.
Even though I expected this to feel different, I accept that this is where we are right now.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: We are in the same house and not quite together.
Side of Eye: The parallel life that has built up without us noticing.
Under Eye: The loneliness of not being known by the person who is supposed to know me.
Under Nose: The conversations that stay at the surface because I don't know how to go deeper.
Chin: The gap between the relationship I thought we had and the one we are in.
Collarbone: The loneliness of not being able to tell them I am lonely.
Under Arm: Missing the version of us that used to be there.
Top of Head: How quiet it has become between us.
Reframe to positive
Eyebrow: Relationships drift. They can also come back.
Side of Eye: Naming the distance is the first step to crossing it.
Under Eye: I am still here. They are still here.
Under Nose: One small reach toward each other is still possible.
Chin: Loneliness inside a relationship is common. It does not mean it is fixed.
Collarbone: I am allowed to want more connection. That is a reasonable thing to want.
Under Arm: Something can shift. Small things shift larger things.
Top of Head: We built this. We can build something else.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
